author: @icebluscorpion
text: You can go further and ditch the 555 timer get a relay the two separated contacts to build two separated circuits. The one switch (NO) is attached with the julethief. And the second relay switch (NC) is in series with the relay coil and the relay will buzz happily away like a buzzer. Note both switches have to be electrically separated they are two different circuits the are only mechanically connected in the relay.

author: @newmonengineering
text: You can ommit the 555 timer in the relay circuit. Feed the relay coil through the normally closed contact. By doing that, the relay will turn on in the off state, and off as soon as the contact breaks. It's commonly called a relay buzzer circuit. If it is controlling different voltages you need a dpdt but if it's the same you can use a dpst.  It has to be a double pole with a nc and no connection. Normally Closed and Normally Open.

replies.author: @NorthernKitty
replies.text: I realize this is 3 years after you asked, but as I understand it the voltage will be limited by what the LED draws.  I know they warn against letting this go "open circuit" as the voltage will spike very high across the transistor.  (And I think this is why it's important that he mentions the battery MUST supply less than the LED needs, so that the transistor shuts off and recycles everything.)  If so, I would think it wise to design "over-voltage protection" into the circuit.  Perhaps the simulations don't account for an upper limit on the LED?  But in reality, I think the LED just happily draws what it needs and stops there.  Someone please feel free to correct me if I'm wrong, otherwise I hope this is helpful.

replies.author: @joeyocom5087
replies.text:
This is an oscillating circuit...do you know what that means?
A transformer is constant flow , thus drains the cell faster

replies.author: @epickiller30
replies.text: Thank you very much. Is it possible to explain how an oscillating circuit drains a cell less than a constant current one?

replies.author: @joeyocom5087
replies.text:
epickiller30 If you listen to the teacher on this video, he explains how the circuit works.
~~~
By using short "taps" of current that makes the LED blink, instead of being on constantly,
thus NOT using current in the time  between oscillations
A flashing light uses less current than one that glows continuously...get it now?

author: @billspradlin9619
text: Isn't the Diode drawn in the wrong direction? Because when the switch is closed the current will go through the diode not the coil. This is similar to a diode on a relay coil and normally the cathode of the diode is towards the positive rail. This type of arrangement is usually called the flyback diode. I could be wrong.

author: @emcgon
text: Nice explanation - thanks.  However, in the first diagram (beginning at 3:24) I think you are showing the LED the wrong way around.  The back-EMF induced across the coil when you open the switch will be more positive at the switch side than at the 1.5V rail side, so you will need to connect the LED the other way around for it to light.

author: @420Rabbits
text: Great video. Is this sort of related to https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/RLC_circuit ?

replies.author: @AshleyMillsTube
replies.text: New Wave Agriculture it's related in that they are both oscillators but in the joule thief case there is no explicit capacitor so the resonance is entirely because of the inductive coupling modulated by the transistor as a switch. In RLC circuits the resonant frequency is determined by the pass back and forth of energy between the capacitor and the inductor, but in any continuously resonating RLC circuit you will need some way of switching too to keep feeding the resonance otherwise the resonance dies out very quickly like an unpushed swing.

author: @danajohnson3799
text: It's always difficult to know who your audience is on You Tube and their level of understanding. All in all, a very good operational description of the "Joule Thief" circuit. The only comment I would make is to avoid the use of acronyms such as EMF (Electromotive Force) without explaining what EMF is. If you use electromotive force in your presentation then counter electromotive force should also be explained and used, otherwise just use voltage. It's a small criticism, which indicates how good your presentation was. Thanks Ashley!

replies.author: @AshleyMillsTube
replies.text: Dana Johnson thanks Dana, good point about the acronyms, so easy just to assume people know or even overlook it entirely

replies.author: @BrentLeVasseur
replies.text: Wow! So you have actually healed graphene using cold plasma? That’s like a Nobel Prize level accomplishment. Why haven’t you demonstrated this or published any papers? By the way, if it does work as you claim then what you have demonstrated is known as ‘negative entropy’ which is the second law of thermodynamics working in reverse! That’s bigger than Relativity in terms of its impact on physics.

author: @mkhamlach
text: So Goood. For several time I want to build a joule thief without know how it is work. Thanks Ash. I want to order the stuff to build a joule thief,could you tell what I have to order? Resistance, ferrit coil.... thanks

replies.author: @AshleyMillsTube
replies.text:
Mo Kha you need some copper winding wire, 0.25 or 0.5mm, a nail, a 2n2222 transistor, an LED, a breadboard, some joining wire, and a 1k resistor, but other values of resistor will work

instead of the nail you can use a ferrite rod or a ferrite toroid, if you do use either of the latter then the number of turns needed will be much less, because the ferrite concentrates the magnetic flux much more readily than the nail does

if you use a toroid for example, then you only need to do about 12 turns on either side of it to make the circuit work.

replies.author: @mkhamlach
replies.text: Thanks Ashley I will order these stuff. Very good video

author: @Robonza
text: I guess if you use a germanium transistor the voltage could go much lower?

replies.author: @AshleyMillsTube
replies.text: Robonza anything that lowers the gate voltage will allow for a lower voltage driving of the circuit. there are some very sensitive field effect transistor's out there that trigger just from ambient RF, so there are some useful directions to go

replies.author: @Robonza
replies.text: Ashley Mills I don't think you can get a gate (fet) voltage lower than a base voltage (germanium bipolar). Can you name a part for me to look at?

replies.author: @AshleyMillsTube
replies.text:
i saw this article the other day http://www.aldinc.com/ald_zerothresholdmosfet.php

but just in general, if you take any mosfet, the accumulated charge will often cause the mosfet to be on and you have to explicitly pull it to ground just to stop it from being spuriously on, I'm looking into this at the moment and how this can be controlled to create a lower effective voltage
